perturbation.original_text: Contractor shall maintain workers compensation insurance in the amount required by statute, comprehensive general liability insurance with coverage of at least one million dollars ($1,000,000) and professional errors and omissions insurance for bodily injury, property damage or other losses with coverage of at least one million dollars ($1,000,000), in connection with the provision of Services by Contractor pursuant to the terms of this Agreement. At Company's request, Contractor shall provide Company with certificates or other acceptable evidence of insurance or self-insurance evidencing the above coverage and shall provide Company with prompt written notice of any material change.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor shall maintain general liability insurance in connection with the provision of Services by Contractor pursuant to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific types and minimum coverage amounts for insurance, the contract fails to ensure adequate protection for the Company, and potentially violates Arizona's minimum insurance requirements for businesses operating within the state.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Arizona Revised Statutes § 23-901 et seq. (Workers' Compensation) and potentially other regulations depending on the specific services provided and industry standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: Arizona law mandates workers' compensation insurance for most employers (A.R.S. § 23-901 et seq.). By omitting specific requirements for workers' compensation and liability insurance, the modified text could contradict Arizona's requirements, placing the Company at legal risk if the contractor does not maintain adequate insurance, or any at all.

(here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Arizona Revised Statutes Title 23 - Labor § 23-901 - Definitions Universal Citation: AZ Rev Stat § 23-901 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 23-901. Definitions In this chapter, unless the context otherwise requires: 1. " Award" means the finding or decision of an administrative law judge or the commission as to the amount of compensation or benefit due an injured employee or the dependents of a deceased employee. 2. " Client" means an individual, association, company, firm, partnership, corporation or any other legally recognized entity that is subject to this chapter and that enters into a professional employer agreement with a professional employer organization. 3. " Co-employee" means every person employed by an injured employee's employer. 4. " Commission" means the industrial commission of Arizona. 5. " Compensation" means the compensation and benefits provided by this chapter. 6. " Employee" , " workman" , " worker" and " operative" means: (a) Every person in the service of this state or a county, city, town, municipal corporation or school district, including regular members of lawfully constituted police and fire departments of cities and towns, whether by election, appointment or contract of hire. (b) Every person in the service of any employer subject to this chapter, including aliens and minors legally or illegally allowed to work for hire, but not including a person whose employment is both: (i) Casual. (ii) Not in the usual course of the trade, business or occupation of the employer. (c) Lessees of mining property and the lessees' emp
perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits the requirement for workers' compensation insurance and specific coverage amounts for general liability and professional errors and omissions insurance, which contradicts Arizona law mandating workers' compensation insurance for most employers (A.R.S. § 23-901 et seq.). The original text ensures adequate protection for the Company, which the modified text removes, potentially placing the Company at legal risk if the contractor does not maintain adequate insurance. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'employee' in the context of Arizona Revised Statutes Title 23, which pertains to labor and includes definitions relevant to workers' compensation, suggesting that the state mandates coverage for those who qualify as employees under the statute.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Arizona, without regard to the conflict of laws provisions thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Company, without regard to the conflict of laws provisions thereof.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the governing law to the "laws of the Company" (Clear Capital) the agreement attempts to circumvent established legal jurisdictions, potentially violating consumer protection laws of Arizona where Clear Capital operates. This is because a private entity cannot dictate the governing law of a contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles and Arizona Consumer Protection Laws
perturbation.law_explanation: A.R.S. § 44-1522 outlines unlawful practices in business. Specifying that 'laws of the Company' govern the contract could be interpreted as an attempt to bypass state consumer protection laws, creating a legal contradiction because it implies the company's internal policies supersede legal statutes, harming the consumer protections.

Previous Next 44-1522. Unlawful practices; intended interpretation of provisions A. The act, use or employment by any person of any deception, deceptive or unfair act or practice, fraud, false pretense, false promise, misrepresentation, or concealment, suppression or omission of any material fact with intent that others rely on such concealment, suppression or omission, in connection with the sale or advertisement of any merchandise whether or not any person has in fact been misled, deceived or damaged thereby, is declared to be an unlawful practice. B. The violation of chapter 9, article 16 or chapter 19, article 1 of this title is declared to be an unlawful practice and subject to enforcement under this article. C. It is the intent of the legislature, in construing subsection A, that the courts may use as a guide interpretations given by the federal trade commission and the federal courts to 15 United States Code sections 45, 52 and 55(a)(1). perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the governing law of Arizona with the "laws of the Company," creating a legal contradiction. This is because a private entity cannot dictate the governing law of a contract. A.R.S. § 44-1522, as described in Snippet 2, addresses unlawful practices, and specifying the company's laws as governing law could bypass state consumer protection laws. The changed text removes a legal protection afforded by Arizona law.

perturbation.original_text: Except as otherwise specified below, all actions, disputes, claims and controversies under common law, statutory law or in equity of any type or nature whatsoever, whether arising before or after the date of this Agreement, and whether directly or indirectly Page 9 of 10 relating to: (a) this Agreement and/or any amendments and addenda hereto, or the breach, invalidity or termination hereof; (b) any previous or subsequent agreement between the parties; and/or (c) any other relationship, transaction or dealing between the parties (collectively the "Disputes"), will be subject to and resolved by binding arbitration pursuant to the Arbitration Rules of U.S. Arbitration & Mediation, (www.usam.com).
perturbation.changed_text: All actions, disputes, claims and controversies under this Agreement will be subject to and resolved by binding arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'common law, statutory law or in equity' the modified text attempts to restrict the scope of potential claims to only those explicitly within the contract, limiting consumers' ability to pursue legal remedies available under state and federal laws. Further, removing the listed reasons of possible disputes limits the ability of claims.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) 9 U.S. Code § 2 and state laws ensuring access to legal remedies.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S. Code § 2) allows for arbitration agreements but does not permit the elimination of rights and remedies granted by statutory or common law. Removing the reference to these legal frameworks contradicts the spirit and intent of laws designed to protect parties in contractual agreements, limiting potential claims.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes essential information, specifically the scope of potential claims, thereby creating a legal loophole. By omitting 'common law, statutory law or in equity,' the altered text restricts claims to only those explicitly within the contract, potentially limiting consumers' ability to pursue legal remedies available under state and federal laws. This omission contradicts the intent of the Federal Arbitration Act, as explained in the Law Explanation, which aims to allow arbitration agreements without eliminating rights and remedies granted by statutory or common law. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 9 U.S. Code § 2, states that arbitration agreements should be 'valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract.' The original text acknowledged these grounds, while the modified text does not, making it likely to fail legal scrutiny.
